AK.notes: Alaska's local government structure is unique, featuring boroughs as county-equivalents, including organized, unorganized, and consolidated city-borough types, along with census areas. Municipalities primarily consist of cities and city-borough consolidated governments. The state has no township governments or independent school districts, instead relying on dependent public school systems categorized by borough, city, or regional educational attendance area.

LA.notes: Louisiana's county-equivalents are known as parishes, typically governed by police juries or parish councils, with some consolidated parish-municipal governments. Municipalities are categorized as cities, towns, and villages. The state features parish and city school districts, alongside various dependent educational and special purpose districts that are often not counted as separate governments.

census_terms: alabama gulf coast convention and visitor bureau
AL.notes: Alabama is entirely covered by county governments and has no township governments or dependent public school systems. Municipalities are classified as cities (2,000+ inhabitants, categorized into eight population classes) or towns (smaller). Public schools are administered by either county or city boards of education, and numerous special districts provide specific services.

AZ.notes: Arizona's entire area is encompassed by county governments, and it features various municipal types including cities, towns, and general municipalities, notably having no township governments. The state recognizes numerous specific school district types as governments, such as common, unified, and community college districts. Additionally, special districts and authorities are counted as governments, while certain tuition financing districts, dependent public school systems, and educational cooperatives are not.

AR.notes: Arkansas counties, governed by a Quorum Court, encompass the entire state, with some divided into two judicial districts. Municipalities are classified by population as first-class cities, second-class cities, or incorporated towns, and no township governments exist. All public education is provided by a single type of school government known as "school districts," and no dependent school systems or other educational entities are counted as separate governments.

CA.notes: California's governmental landscape includes county governments, notably the unique San Francisco city and county, but no township governments. Municipalities are designated as either charter cities or general law cities. The state features diverse public school systems, encompassing elementary, high school, unified, and community college districts, alongside other special districts like air pollution control districts.

CO.notes: Colorado's area is covered by county governments and consolidated city-county governments, the latter operating primarily as cities and counted as municipal governments alongside traditional cities and towns. The state has no township governments or dependent public school systems. Separate public school system governments include school districts, junior college districts, and affiliated junior college districts.

CT.notes: Connecticut's county governments were abolished in 1960 and are not counted as separate governmental units, though their boundaries remain. Subcounty general purpose governments include municipalities (cities and boroughs) and towns, with towns providing services typically associated with counties and municipalities in other states. Only regional school districts are counted as separate governments, alongside dependent city and town school systems.

DE.notes: Delaware's entire area is covered by county governments, with "levy courts" and "county councils" serving as administrative bodies. Municipal governments include "cities," "towns," and "villages," and there are no organized "township governments." All school districts are "reorganized school districts," and special districts encompass various "authorities," "district libraries," "housing authorities," and "municipal electric companies."

census_terms: university of the district of columbia
district of columbia water and sewer authority
DC.notes: The District of Columbia uniquely performs municipal, county-equivalent, and state-type government functions itself, lacking separate county or independent municipal governments. It operates dependent public school systems and universities instead of independent school districts. Specific special districts, such as the Washington Metropolitan Area Transit Authority, are often termed 'authorities'.

FL.notes: Florida's local governments include standard county governments and municipal governments, locally known as cities, towns, and villages. The state does not have township governments. Public school systems are categorized as county school systems or Florida college system districts, and a variety of special districts are also authorized and counted as separate governments.

GA.notes: Georgia's county governments encompass the entire state, though specific consolidated and unified governments in some areas are counted as municipal entities. Municipalities are known as cities and towns, while the state lacks township governments. Public school systems consist of county boards of education, independent city school districts, and independent school districts, with no new independent districts creatable since 1983.

census_terms: hawaii housing finance and development corporation
natural energy laboratory of hawaii authority
pacific international space center for exploration system (pisces)
research corporation of the university of hawaii
urban, rural, agricultural, and conservation district (land use commission)
HI.notes: Hawaii has a unique government structure: it recognizes county governments, but Kalawao County is an adjunct of the state and Honolulu is classified as a municipal government. It has no independent school districts or township governments, with public education administered by the State Department of Education. Special districts include soil and water conservation districts and the Office of Hawaiian Affairs, with numerous other subordinate agencies and authorities classified as non-governmental.

ID.notes: Idaho's local government structure includes county governments overseen by boards of county commissioners, and municipal governments primarily designated as "cities," with no townships. The state recognizes various school districts, such as elementary, joint, specially chartered, and community college districts, alongside specific special districts like aquifer recharge and ground water districts. While terms like "school subdistricts" and "cooperative service agencies" exist, they are not counted as separate governmental units for Census purposes.

IL.notes: Illinois is entirely covered by county governments, and also features subcounty general purpose governments comprising municipal (cities, villages, and incorporated towns) and township governments. Township governments exist in 84 of the 102 counties. The state recognizes various specific types of school districts as distinct governments, including common, community college, community high, community unit, non-high, special charter, and township high school districts.

census_terms: bi-state development agency of the missouri-illinois metropolitan district
exhibition center and recreational facility district
MO.notes: Missouri features standard county governments, with St. Louis as a unique independent city. Subcounty general purpose governments include municipalities (cities, towns, villages, categorized by population) and townships, which exist in specific classes of counties. School districts are diverse, encompassing seven-director, urban, metropolitan, community college, and various special and vocational districts. The state also has a wide array of special districts for services like ambulance, fire protection, and various authorities for transportation and land management.

IN.notes: Indiana organizes its entire area by county governments, with Marion County and Indianapolis consolidated into a single city-county government. Subcounty general-purpose governments include municipal (cities and towns, classified by population) and township governments. The state has a wide array of school districts, all legally termed 'school corporations', and numerous special districts covering services like parks, building authorities, conservancy, and drainage.

census_terms: benefited recreational lake and water quality district
IA.notes: Iowa has a single layer of county governments, which encompass the entire state. Subcounty general purpose governments consist of cities (formerly including towns). Townships exist but are classified as administrative subdivisions of counties and are not counted as separate governments by the Census Bureau. School districts include community, consolidated, independent, and merged school areas. Special districts are numerous and varied, serving purposes from airports and fire protection to water, recreation, and emergency services.

census_terms: unified government of wyandotte county and kansas city
kansas coalition of innovative school district
KS.notes: Kansas's county-equivalents are generally 'county governments,' but two 'unified governments' are counted as municipal governments. Municipalities are called 'cities' and are further categorized into three classes based on population. The state also has 'township governments' that cover most areas not within first or second-class cities. School districts are primarily 'unified school districts,' but 'community college districts' and 'municipal universities' are also recognized.

KY.notes: Kentucky's local government structure includes county governments (with two unique municipal governments that function as county-equivalents for Census purposes), and several types of municipal governments like first-class and home rule-class cities. The state has no township or dependent public school systems, but has both county and independent (city) school districts. Special districts are numerous and include various service-specific units like ambulance, drainage, fire, hospital, and housing authorities.

ME.notes: Maine's county-equivalents are called county governments, which share duties with plantations. Subcounty general purpose governments consist of cities, towns, and plantations, with towns and plantations performing most local services. School districts have various names, including school administrative districts and regional school units, while many school systems are dependent on municipal or town governments.

census_terms: the bel air special taxing area
bowling green and robert's place special taxing area
friendship height and “the hills” special tax district
village of drummond special taxing area
maryland-national capital park and planning commission
auxiliary and academic facility bond authority
canal place preservation and development authority
MD.notes: Maryland's government structure is dominated by county governments, with Baltimore City being a unique independent municipality. The state lacks independent school districts and township governments, instead having dependent public school systems and a variety of special districts for specific services.

MA.notes: Massachusetts uses both county and town governments, with the latter having powers similar to cities. The state features regional school districts, vocational-technical school districts, and various special districts for services like fire, water, housing, and economic development. Some county governments have been abolished, with their functions potentially absorbed by cities and towns or regional councils of governments.

census_terms: county water, sewer, and sewer and garbage disposal system
MI.notes: Michigan's government structure includes county, municipal (cities and villages), and township governments. Villages are notable for being included within township governments. School districts are categorized by size and creation method, while a broad array of special districts and authorities exist, many formed through intergovernmental agreements or contracts for specific services.

MN.notes: Minnesota's county-equivalents are county governments, which encompass the entire state. Municipalities are referred to as cities and are categorized into four classes by population. Townships are interchangeably called towns and do not cover the entire area of the counties, with some cities and all unorganized territory existing outside their jurisdiction. School districts are primarily common, independent, and special types, with a unique instance of a tribal council administering a school. The state also has a wide variety of special districts with specific functions and governance structures, including airport, ambulance, hospital, and housing authorities, as well as several metropolitan area commissions.

census_terms: northeast mississippi regional water supply district
MS.notes: Mississippi has county governments covering the entire state, and municipalities are categorized as cities, towns, or villages. There are no township governments. School districts are diverse, including county-wide, consolidated, municipal separate, and special municipal separate types, along with junior colleges counted as governments. Numerous special districts exist for various services, ranging from air ambulance and drainage to housing and port commissions.

MT.notes: Montana is fully covered by county governments except for two consolidated city-counties (Anaconda-Deer Lodge and Butte-Silver Bow) which are classified as municipalities. Municipalities are divided into three classes of cities and a town category based on population, while townships do not exist as active governments. School districts are highly structured into elementary, high school, community college, and K-12 systems, complemented by a variety of single-purpose special districts.

census_terms: joint electric power, sewerage and solid waste disposal, and water distribution agency
NE.notes: Nebraska's county governing bodies are boards of commissioners or, in the 25 counties with active township governments, boards of supervisors. Subcounty general-purpose governments include cities of four population classes, villages governed by boards of trustees, and the unified legal concept of a municipal county. School systems include three classes of school districts, community college areas, educational service units, and unified school systems.

UT.notes: Utah governs through counties and municipalities (cities and towns), while metro townships are classified as municipal-type governments. The state features a variety of independent special districts and school districts, but it contains no township governments or dependent public school systems. Many local service functions are managed by districts that may be classified as subordinate agencies if governed ex officio by county or municipal legislative bodies.

census_terms: greater huntington park and recreation district
greenbrier, monroe, and pocahontas multi-county economic development authority
WV.notes: West Virginia's county governments are governed by county commissions, while municipal governments are classified by population into cities, towns, and villages. Each county also functions as an independent school district governed by an elected county board of education. The state also utilizes a variety of specialized single-county and multicounty special district governments, such as public service districts and conservation districts.

census_terms: public inland lake protection and rehabilitation district
WI.notes: Wisconsin government units are categorized into counties, municipalities (cities and villages), and townships (towns). School districts, including technical college districts, are counted as separate governments, while many other specialized entities like drainage or stadium districts are also recognized as independent special district governments if they meet specific governance criteria.

WY.notes: Wyoming government units consist of county governments, municipal governments (cities and towns), and various independent special districts. The state contains no township governments or dependent school systems. Many local entities, such as boards of cooperative services and local improvement districts, are classified as subordinate agencies rather than independent governments.

census_terms: california-nevada super speed ground transportation commission
NV.notes: Nevada's county governments encompass the entire state except for Carson City, which is classified as a municipal government. Municipal governments consist of incorporated cities and towns, while "unincorporated towns" are treated as county adjuncts rather than independent governments. The state's 17 school districts are independent governments coterminous with the 16 counties and Carson City.

NH.notes: In New Hampshire, subcounty general purpose governments consist of cities and towns, with towns officially treated as municipalities but counted separately as towns by the Census Bureau. Unincorporated areas are organized into gores, grants, purchases, unorganized locations, and unorganized townships. Special districts include conservation districts, village districts (or precincts), and regional transit or waste districts.

NJ.notes: New Jersey is entirely covered by county governments governed by boards of county commissioners. Subcounty governments consist of municipal governments (boroughs, cities, towns, and villages) and township governments, which are counted separately in Census statistics despite having similar powers. School districts are classified as Type 1 (dependent) or Type 2 (independent), alongside various joint educational agencies and authorized special districts.

census_terms: eastern new mexico water utility authority
lower rio grande public water works authority
NM.notes: New Mexico is entirely encompassed by county governments, with Los Alamos designated as an incorporated county. Municipalities are categorized as cities, towns, or villages, while townships do not exist. Special districts include unique agricultural and historical entities such as acequias and community land grants.

NY.notes: In New York, county governments cover the entire state except for the five boroughs of New York City, which is classified as a municipal government. Subcounty governments consist of cities, villages, and towns, with towns classified as township governments rather than municipalities in Census statistics. Independent school districts are classified into six distinct structural types, while special districts like fire and library districts operate as separate local governments.

census_terms: area mental health, developmental disabilities, and substance abuse area authority
NC.notes: North Carolina is entirely covered by county governments governed by boards of county commissioners. Municipal governments consist of cities, towns, and incorporated villages with no legal differences, while townships exist only as non-governmental geographic areas for election purposes. There are no independent school districts in the state; instead, all public education units are classified as dependent agencies of county or municipal governments.

ND.notes: In North Dakota, all municipal governments are legally designated as cities and exist outside of township areas. Township governments (including consolidated multi-townships) do not cover the entire state, leaving some areas as unorganized territory. School districts operate as independent public school districts, while special districts span a wide range of utility, safety, and conservation authorities.

OH.notes: Ohio is entirely divided into county governments, which are governed by boards of county commissioners or county councils under home-rule charters. Subcounty areas are divided into municipal governments (classified as cities or villages) and township governments, which are not counted separately if they are coterminous with a municipality. The state also features various independent school districts, community colleges, and diverse special districts ranging from conservancy districts to joint fire and ambulance districts.

census_terms: rural water, sewer, gas, and solid waste management district
OK.notes: Oklahoma's county governments are governed by boards of county commissioners, while its subcounty general purpose governments are classified as cities or towns based on a population threshold of 1,000. The state's independent school systems include independent, elementary, and technology center school districts. Oklahoma also authorizes a wide variety of special districts, including circuit engineering, conservancy, and specialized rural infrastructure districts.

OR.notes: Oregon's local government structure consists of county governments, municipalities (cities, towns, and villages), and various special districts. The state has no township governments or dependent public school systems, but recognizes several distinct types of independent school districts and education service districts. A wide range of single-purpose and multipurpose special districts, including a unique metropolitan service district (Metro), operate as independent governmental units.

PA.notes: Pennsylvania's local governments include counties, municipal governments (consisting of cities, boroughs, and one incorporated town), and townships which are counted as a separate unit type. The state has no dependent public school systems, meaning its school districts and community colleges function as independent governments. Special districts in the state are commonly established as independent municipal authorities to finance and operate public facilities.

RI.notes: Rhode Island has no active county governments, so counties exist only as geographic subdivisions for judicial administration. The state's subcounty general purpose governments consist of cities (counted as municipalities) and towns (counted as town or township governments), both of which have similar powers. The only independent school systems are regional school districts, while all other school systems and various specialized authorities are classified as dependent agencies.

SC.notes: South Carolina has county governments covering the entire state, while its subcounty general purpose governments are municipal governments classified as cities and towns. School systems exist as independent governments (either countywide or independent school districts), while various specialized services are provided by special districts and authorities created under general law or special acts.

census_terms: regional recycling and waste management district
SD.notes: South Dakota's subcounty general purpose governments are categorized as municipal (classified into three population-based groups: first, second, and third class) and township governments. The state features no dependent public school systems, meaning all school districts function as separate, independent governments. Additionally, a wide variety of special districts are authorized, including ambulance, consumers power, and predator control districts.

TN.notes: Tennessee has no township governments, and its entire area is divided into counties except for three combined county-city municipal governments. Only 'special' school districts established before 1982 are counted as independent school district governments, while all other school systems are dependent on the state, county, or municipal governments. The state also authorizes numerous independent special district governments, including various types of airport, housing, port, and utility-related authorities.

census_terms: independent school district junior college district
fire control and prevention and emergency medical services district
TX.notes: Texas is entirely encompassed by county governments governed by commissioners courts. Municipal governments consist of cities, towns, and villages categorized into Type A, B, or C. Special districts and independent school districts (including community college districts) make up the state's independent local government units.

census_terms: unified towns and gores of essex county
VT.notes: Vermont features very limited county governments, with subcounty areas divided into cities, villages, and towns (which the Census Bureau classifies as township governments). Unincorporated and often uninhabited areas known as gores, grants, and unorganized towns exist but are not counted as separate governments. The state also utilizes a wide variety of independent school districts and special districts, including union municipal districts and the unique Unified Towns and Gores of Essex County.

VA.notes: In Virginia, cities exist outside of any county area and are classified as independent incorporated communities counted as municipal governments. Towns are also municipal governments but remain within county boundaries, while counties encompass all other areas. There are no active township or tier-city governments, and all local public school systems except for one unique college are classified as dependent agencies of county or municipal governments.

census_terms: agency created under the interlocal cooperation act
cultural arts, stadium, and convention district
WA.notes: Washington is entirely covered by county governments, governed by boards of commissioners or county councils, with cities and towns serving as municipal governments. School districts are independent governments classified by student enrollment into first- and second-class districts. Special districts are authorized for a wide range of functions, including fire protection, diking, drainage, and air pollution control.
